content: Hi! Has anyone worked on adding spinlocks that save/restore IRQs when being locked/unlocked? I have a small patch to add it that I wouldn't mind trying to upstream, but I figured I should ask around to make sure no one else has gotten to this yet
What are you using it for?

Alice Ryhl said:

What are you using it for?

RVKMS (the rust port of the VKMS kernel driver), specifically for working with vertical blanking where DRM's expectation is that most vertical blanks come from an actual interrupt - meaning all of the vblank related spinlocks in KMS disable/enable spinlocks by default
(to be honest I was a bit surprised it wasn't added already, I see far more spinlocks being grabbed under interrupts then ones not being grabbed under interrupts)
I don't think anyone has put one together yet.
ah cool, honestly it's simple enough I might just send the patch onto the mailing list today or tomorrow.
Be aware of this
oh dear haha, that might explain why this hasn't been upstreamed yet
Either we make it unsafe or we say we rely on klint to avoid the bad case.

that seems kind of painful tbh, I'd kinda rather not make something this core to a lot of drivers need to be unsafe.
Why not have it so that we treat IRQ state as a separate object that disables interrupts once you get the object - and then you have to pass that state object to lock()/unlock() whenever you want to grab an IRQ that's known to be grabbed with interrupts disabled?
You will have a similar issue when you have two local_irq_save(), unless you track the nest level of those in a per-thread or per-cpu variable, right?

I'm curious why we'd need to track the nesting level? If you disable IRQs once and then try to save the IRQ disable flag again, isn't the second set of saved flags going to just be a no-op when we restore them anyway?
We would need to at the very least make sure that whatever guard we return from the spinlock borrows from the actual IRQ guard
or hm, I guess that doesn't stop someone from just dropping the irq guards in the wrong order

Say:

// IRQ is enabled
let guard1 = irq_save();
// IRQ is disabled.
let guard2 = irq_save();
drop(guard1);
// IRQ is enabled while guard2 exists.

Lyude said:

or hm, I guess that doesn't stop someone from just dropping the irq guards in the wrong order

Yeah..
this is definitely a common enough usecase though I'm really hesistant to just rely on unsafe unless we know there's a better solution on the horizon
oh wow someone just suggested a good idea: what about using closures for this

Maybe a closure API, like:

fn with_irq_save<F: FnOnce()>(f: F) {
    let mut flags;
    unsafe { local_irq_save(&flags); }
    f();
    unsafe { local_irq_restore(&flags); }
}

@Boqun Feng so I think I'm starting to wrap my head around variance. I assume declaring IrqDisabled like this makes it invariant over lifetime 'a:

pub struct IrqDisabled<'a>(PhantomData<*mut &'a ()>);

correct?
that should be correct, but normally you would use PhantomData<fn(&'a ()) -> &'a ()>, since that would still be Send (though in our case I guess we would like it to not be).

hm, I guess I'm not sure if we'd really want this? At least if I'm understanding correctly, we probably wouldn't want it to be invariant over the lifetime considering that we're already expecting interrupts to be disabled over the lifetime of IrqDisabled - and the only safe way of creating one (with_irqs_disabled()) enforces that through virtue of being a closure interface
at first I only stated the question without actually having any preference of invariant/covariant, but now I think I have an argument in favor of invariance:

scratch that, I just tested my hypothesis and turns out I was wrong :sweat_smile:

what I thought would be problematic was this:

    pub fn foo(&self, _: IrqDisabled<'_>) -> &'_ usize {
        &self.0
    }

I thought it would desugar to this:

    pub fn foo<'a>(&'a self, _: IrqDisabled<'a>) -> &'a usize {
        &self.0
    }

but it actually desugars to this:

    pub fn foo<'a, 'b>(&'a self, _: IrqDisabled<'b>) -> &'a usize {
        &self.0
    }

Gotcha, so I guess I'll just keep it covariant for the time being (which to me makes sense, since we definitely want to be able to hand down IrqDisabled to other functions so they don't need to redundantly save/restore IRQ flags.
it being invariant doesn't prevent you from doing that though
ah gotcha. yeah I'm still wrapping my head around this a bit loll
yeah no worries
wait - is it being invariant in this case just saying that it needs its own lifetime?
like - it can't be 'a if &self is &'a self
no not necessarily
being invariant means that you can assign IrqDisabled<'a> to IrqDisabled<'b> if and only if 'a == 'b
if IrqDisabled is covariant, you can assign IrqDisabled<'a> to IrqDisabled<'b> if and only if 'a: 'b, so if 'a outlives 'b.

but I guess one could expect that IrqDisabled is covariant, since it is a token that signifies that IRQs are disabled. you are allowed to shorten the lifetime on that, since IRQs will at least be disabled until it is dropped.

the way IRQs are dis/reen-abled is the following:

pub fn with_irqs_disabled<T, F>(cb: F) -> T
where
    F: FnOnce(IrqDisabled<'_>) -> T,
{
    // SAFETY: FFI call with no special requirements
    let flags = unsafe { bindings::local_irq_save() };

    let ret = cb(IrqDisabled(PhantomData));

    // SAFETY: `flags` comes from our previous call to local_irq_save
    unsafe { bindings::local_irq_restore(flags) };

    ret
}

And you would lock an SpinLockIrq within the cb. So

with_irq_disabled(|irq| {
    let guard = lock.lock_with(irq);
    // use guard here
});
Oh, so the token's existence is a sign that you are within an IRQ-disabled closure, not an IRQ-disable guard

// !Send and !Sync
struct IrqDisabled(PhantomData<*mut ()>);


impl IrqDisabled {
    pub fn unsafe new() -> Self {
        debug_assert!(unsafe { !bindings::irq_disabled() });
        IrqDisabled(PhantomData)
    }
}

impl<...> Lock<...> {
    pub fn lock_with(&self, &IrqDisabled) -> Guard<..> { ... }
}

/// # Safety
/// Callers need to ensure `cb` returns with the same irq disabled state.
pub unsafe fn with_irqs_disabled(cb: for<'a> impl FnOnce(&'a IrqDisabled) -> T) -> T {
    let irq_save = unsafe { bindings::local_irq_save() };

    // SAFETY: IRQ is disabled by above.
    let id = unsafe { IrqDisabled::new() };
    let t = cb(&id);
    drop(id);

    // SAFETY: Pairs with the above `local_irq_save()`, and per function safety requirement, the irq disable state is the same as it is after the above `local_irq_save()`.
    unsafe { bindings::local_irq_restore(irq_save); }

    t
}

    // SAFETY: IRQ is disabled by above.
    let id = unsafe { IrqDisabled::new() };
    let t = cb(&id);
    drop(id);

    // SAFETY: Pairs with the above `local_irq_save()`, and per function safety requirement, the irq disable state is the same as it is after the above `local_irq_save()`.
    unsafe { bindings::local_irq_restore(irq_save); }

So, before I explain I'm going to list some of the important facts to keep in mind while reading this:

  • Interrupt disables can be and commonly are nested
  • If you see "IRQ ??", this means "IRQs may or may not be on in this context".

  • There are two different types of contexts where interrupts can be disabled:
    Context A: In response to a hardware interrupt - in this context, interrupts can -never- be turned back on
    Context B: After being manually disabled from a state where they were previously on - interrupts can be re-enabled

  • Any kind of lock, say a spinlock, which may be acquired in a no-IRQ context must -always- be acquired in a no-irq context

  • Any kind of lock acquired in a no-IRQ context must be dropped before interrupts are enabled. In other words, the lifetime of its guard is tied to the lifetime of IrqDisabled. We'll call this "the spinlock rule".
  • We will make one of the safety requirements of IrqDisabled::new() be "the caller must never create multiple of these tokens within the same scope"

Now recall that we've also defined a SpinLockIrq, in this write-up locking SpinLockIrq will look like this:

  some_spinlockirq.lock_with(&'a irq) -> Guard<'a, …>

Furthermore, we can classify anything in the kernel that relies or changes interrupt state into three groups:

  • IRQs off -> do work -> IRQs off
    This is the simplest one: we know we started with interrupts disabled, so we can't assume we won't break the spinlock rule if we turn them back on since we didn't disable them in the first place. So, "do work" can only contain nested IRQ disables

  • IRQs ?? -> IRQs off -> do work -> IRQs ??
    This is a bit more complicated, but follows the same rule as the previous group. Because interrupts -could- be disabled, we lack enough context to know that turning interrupts back on would not break the spinlock rule. Thus - "do work" can only contain nested IRQ disables.

  • IRQs on -> IRQs off -> do work (can contain IRQs on -> IRQs off) -> IRQs on
    Since we started with IRQs enabled and disabled them ourselves, this means have enough context to know that so long as we drop any spinlocks we acquired after "IRQs off" we can safely re-enable interrupts without breaking the spinlock rule.

I'll start by handling the third group, since this is important to understand for handling the first and second group. We would handle this group using with_irqs_disabled():

// SAFETY: `irq` must be Some() if IRQs are enabled when this function is called, or None otherwise
pub unsafe fn with_irqs_disabled<T>(
  irq: Option<IrqDisabled>,
  cb: impl FnOnce(IrqDisabled) -> T,
) -> (Option<IrqDisabled>, T) {
  …
}

Here, IrqDisabled is passed to the callback by value. But with a caveat - the user is asked to provide an Option<IrqDisabled> which represents the -current- IRQ state as part of the safety contract for with_irqs_disabled. We can also use a debug assertion to verify this at runtime to help catch mistakes.

The advantage of requiring the user pass an IrqDisabled here is simple: the user is required to only ever have one of these tokens in scope if interrupts are disabled, via the safety contract of IrqDisabled::new. And since the user needs to pass their token (if they have one) to this function by value, no spinlock guards which we acquired using the token will be alive once interrupts are enabled. Thus, we don't violate the spinlock rule even with nested calls.

Furthermore, we can require that any function which goes from IRQs off -> IRQs on must take an IrqDisabled. As an example, let's create an imaginary binding for wait_event_lock_interruptible_irq():

pub fn wait_event_lock_interruptible_irq(irq: IrqDisabled, lock: &SpinLockIrq<…>, cb: impl FnOnce()) -> IrqDisabled {
  …
}

Since we've taken an IrqDisabled by value, we can be sure that we've ended the lifetime shared by any Guards which we acquired while interrupts were disabled. We also know that either we were the ones who disabled interrupts originally, or that any earlier context which disabled interrupts has already dropped any Guards which relied on interrupts being disabled. Thus, we can confirm we haven't violated the spinlock rule.

Since this would kind of be a pain ergonomically, we can forgo this trouble entirely for the first and second group I previously mentioned - and even provide a safe function in this case:

pub fn with_irqs_always_disabled<T>(cb: impl FnOnce(&IrqDisabled) -> T) -> T {
  …
}

Since this function only passes IrqDisabled by reference, we can assume the following:

  • Whether IRQs were enabled or disabled when this function was called is irrelevant - as it is impossible with safe code to re-enable them within cb() since we only have a reference to IrqDisabled.
  • Since we can nest IRQ disables, with_irqs_always_disabled() can also be safely nested since IRQ enables are impossible within cb()
  • Thus, the spinlock rule cannot be violated with this function using safe code :)

Keeping in mind we only have two ways of creating an owned IrqDisabled - with_irqs_disabled() and IrqDisabled::new() which are both unsafe functions. Now, we could make with_irqs_disabled() say in its safety contract that any function calling this must:

  • Be unsafe itself
  • accept a Option<IrqDisabled> and pass it directly to with_irqs_disabled(). Then, with_irqs_disabled() would return Option<IrqDisabled> which should be equivalent to the input irq argument (e.g. if we pass Some(IrqDisabled) in, it should give us Some(IrqDisabled) out - and vice versa for None)
  • We would also require that at the end of with_irqs_disabled() the state of interrupts must match what it was when the function was originally called. So if it does re-enable interrupts, it's required to disable them by the time it returns
  • And finally, require that any function calling with_irqs_disabled() needs to return the result of with_irqs_disabled() (e.g. the Option<IrqDisabled>).

For IrqDisabled::new() - we could simply make it in the safety contract that any function using this must not have disabled interrupts itself unless it follows the safety contract of with_irqs_disabled().

So - going back to your example, if re_enable_irqs() enables interrupts through with_irqs_disabled() - then this code would be in violation of the contract unless it takes a Option<IrqDisabled> by value and returns it, and by the safety contract that value must match the interrupt state at the start of the function.

The advantage of this complexity is that with all of this, because we require a function that re-enables interrupts to consume a Option<IrqDisabled> from its parent context - we would be ending the lifetime of any resources that were acquired using a reference to IrqDisabled. Which means that (hopefully I'm not misunderstanding how rust handles this) we would be forcing spinlocks held under interrupt to be dropped by the time interrupts are turned on. And trying to use nesting to avoid this requirement would just simply be in violation of that safety contract because of the need for irq: Option<IrqDisabled> to represent the starting interrupt state.

Then of course, the far more common case of the safe function with_irqs_disabled_always() which simply deals in references and doesn't care about the starting interrupt state would only have references to IrqDisabled. Thus, since we can only generate an IrqDisabled token by-value using unsafe code, that would make it impossible to generate the Option<IrqDisabled> for with_irqs_disabled() without violating the safety contract of with_irqs_disabled(). Since the only other way of creating the token requires that the function not control interrupts itself (via the requirements of IrqDisabled::new(), or follow the safety contract of with_irqs_disabled().

So there is a bridge can connect your design with what I have, we can define a 

pub struct IrqDisabledFlag(usize, NotThreadSafe);

whose new function is a unsafe one:

impl IrqDisabledFlag {
    /// Disable the interrupt and return the state.
    ///
    /// # Safety
    /// `drop` must be called with the same interrupt state as `new`.
    pub fn unsafe new() -> Self {
        let mut flags = 0;
        unsafe { local_irq_save(&mut flags); };
        Self(flags, NotThreadSafe)
    }
}

Usually this safety requirement would look weird, but we can actually check this at runtime:

impl Drop for IrqDisabledFlag {
    fn drop(&mut self) {
        #[cfg(SOME_DEBUG)]
        {
            let mut flags = 0;
            unsafe { local_irq_save(&mut flags); }
            unsafe { local_irq_restore(flags); }
            assert_eq!(flags, self.0);
        }
        unsafe { local_irq_restore(self.0); }
    }
}
In this way, the correctness nesting is guaranteed by the safety requirement of IrqDisabledFlag::new().

And we can implement the InterruptDisabled in the POC with IrqDisabledFlags if we have PerCpu support in Rust:

pub struct InterruptDisabled(NotThreadSafe);

static IRQ_DISABLED_FLAG: PerCpu<Option<IrqDisabledFlags>> = PerCpu::new(None);
static IRQ_DISABLED_LEVEL: PerCpu<isize> = PerCpu::new(0);

impl InterruptDisabled {
    pub fn new() -> Self {
        let irq_disabled_flag = unsafe { IrqDisabledFlag::new() };
        if IRQ_DISABLE_LEVEL.inc_return() == 1 {
            (*IRQ_DISABLED_FLAG.deref_mut()) = Some(irq_disabled_flag);
        } else {
            forget(irq_disabled_flag);
        }
        Self(NotThreadSafe)
    }
}

impl Drop for InterruptDisabled {
    fn drop(&mut self) {
        if IRQ_DISABLED_LEVEL.dec_return() == 0 {
            IRQ_DISABLED_FLAG.deref_mut().take(); // IrqDisabledFlag will drop and restore the state
        }
    }
}
After this, we will have more flexible interrupt control in Rust.

From: https://0xax.gitbooks.io/linux-insides/content/Interrupts/linux-interrupts-1.html

The IST or Interrupt Stack Table is a new mechanism in the x86_64. It is used as an alternative to the legacy stack-switch mechanism. Previously the x86 architecture provided a mechanism to automatically switch stack frames in response to an interrupt. The IST is a modified version of the x86 Stack switching mode. This mechanism unconditionally switches stacks when it is enabled and can be enabled for any interrupt in the IDT entry related with the certain interrupt (we will soon see it). From this we can understand that IST is not necessary for all interrupts. Some interrupts can continue to use the legacy stack switching mode. The IST mechanism provides up to seven IST pointers in the Task State Segment or TSS which is the special structure which contains information about a process. The TSS is used for stack switching during the execution of an interrupt or exception handler in the Linux kernel. Each pointer is referenced by an interrupt gate from the IDT.

The original problem was in this function:

    pub(crate) fn do_unlocked<U>(&mut self, cb: impl FnOnce() -> U) -> U {
        // SAFETY: The caller owns the lock, so it is safe to unlock it.
        unsafe { B::unlock(self.lock.state.get(), &self.state) };

        let _relock = ScopeGuard::new(||
                // SAFETY: The lock was just unlocked above and is being relocked now.
                unsafe { B::relock(self.lock.state.get(), &mut self.state) });

        cb()
    }

The lock would get dissociated from the InterruptDisabled token back then, so when the condvar called schedule_timeout, interrupts would still be disabled, thereby triggering a bug
you can enable LOCKDEP with the kconfig_add option of kuint

DECLARE_PER_CPU(struct interrupt_disable_state, local_interrupt_disable_state);

static inline void local_interrupt_disable(void)
{
    unsigned long flags;
    int new_count;

    new_count = hardirq_disable_enter();

    if ((new_count & HARDIRQ_DISABLE_MASK) == HARDIRQ_DISABLE_OFFSET) {
        local_irq_save(flags);
        raw_cpu_write(local_interrupt_disable_state.flags, flags);
    }
}

static inline void local_interrupt_enable(void)
{
    int new_count;

    new_count = hardirq_disable_exit();

    if ((new_count & HARDIRQ_DISABLE_MASK) == 0) {
        unsigned long flags;

        if (should_resched(0))

    }
}

OK, let's take common_interrupt as an example.

First in arch/x86/include/asm/idtentry.h, the DECLARE_IDTENTRY_IRQ() macro would call the assembler macro idtentry_irq defined in arch/x86/entry/entry_64.S. And eventually, it will generate an interrupt function asm_common_interrupt. In the generated asm_common_interrupt you can find it eventually call error_return (defined in entry_64.S as well):

asm_common_interrupt:
    ...
    call common_interrupt
    jump error_return

error_return will eventually execute iret.

Now the common_interrupt() function is more related the softirq issue, it's defined in arch/x86/kernel/irq.c by DEFINE_IDTENTRY_IRQ() macro (defined in idtentry.h as well). In most cases, it'll add irq_enter_rcu() and irq_exit_rcu() around the real interrupt handler (__common_interrupt()).

So over all, in a call trace that irq_enter_rcu() and irq_exit_rcu() are involved. The interrupt disabling/enabling are roughly as follow:

// interrupt gate automatically disables interrupts
__common_interrupt():
    common_interrupt():
        irq_enter_rcu();
        __common_interrupt():
            call_irq_handler();
            apic_eoi(); // <- may be called if call_irq_handler() fails
        irq_exit_rcu():
            __irq_exit_rcu():
                 local_irq_disable();
                 // ^ if irq is already disabled, it's fine, since we want to disable
                 // it anyway.
                 invoke_softirq():
                     __do_softirq():
                         handle_softirq():
                             local_irq_enable();
                             // ^ softirq should run with irq enabled,
                             // that's the point
                             <run softirq handlers>
                             local_irq_disable();
    error_return():
        iret // <- Re-enable the irq
So semantically speaking, the local_irq_disable() in __irq_exit_rcu() should not participate the refcounting.
I.e. if we were to define local_irq_disable() as local_interrupt_disable(), we should replace that with a (unpaired) local_irq_save(flags).

I have a feeling it might be better to just keep local_irq_disable() separate from the refcounted api mechanism that rust needs, and not convert it. IOW, I wonder if its really worth the complexity just to reduce the number of APIs.. If we want to unconditionally disable / enable IRQs, we shouldn't pretend to try to track it (especially in already working C code..)

@Andreas Hindborg ^ figured I should probably ask: while I don't currently have a benchmark that explicitly checks the performance gain from BackendInContext, I do actually know that we do see measurable performance differences between refcounted IRQ enable/disable (slower) vs. non-refcounted IRQ enable/disable, and IIRC the bottleneck is the conditional that we had to add for checking the interrupt refcount.
With this being said though, since lock_with() would avoid touching the interrupt refcount - I think it's very likely we'd see pretty much the same performance difference between lock() (slower) vs. lock_with() (faster) since the latter skips the conditional that caused the earlier performance hit.
Does that seem like enough proof, or would you rather I still write up a benchmark?

Notice that in an interrupt handler, where interrupts are disabled (because Linux dosen't support nested interrupts), the refcount could still be 0, so if we still use SpinLockIrq::lock(), it'll go through the interrupt disabling logic and issue the actual irq disable instructions. lock_with() can save the cost of that.

For non-interrupt context, I do not believe this code is going to make a difference. You are incrementing/decrementing a refcount in a cache line that the CPU already has exclusive ownership of.

For interrupt context, I am not aware of the cost of disabling interrupts by writing the interrupt control register, if interrupts are already disabled. But I would assume it is negligible.

Which is why I ask you to measure this. It should be quick enough to do a micro benchmark, but better yet to use this in a real example. If you want, I can put it into the nvme driver. I think there is a place for this patch in there.
It's just a lot of added complexity for something that probably does not make a difference.

+        use crate::interrupt::*;
+        use crate::sync::Arc;

+

+
+        let comp1 = Arc::pin_init(Completion::new(), GFP_KERNEL)?;
+        let comp2= Arc::pin_init(Completion::new(), GFP_KERNEL)?;
+        let comp3 = Arc::pin_init(Completion::new(), GFP_KERNEL)?;

+

I think this is a cleaner design for the lock_with method:

unsafe trait LockWith<C> {
    type ActingBackend: Backend;
}

impl<T: ?Sized, B: Backend> Lock<T, B> {
    pub fn lock_with<'a, C>(&'a self, _: C) -> Guard<'a, T, B::ActingBackend>
    where
        B: LockWith<C>,
        C: 'a, // Lifetimes in the context must outlive the guard.
     { ... }
}

unsafe impl LockWith<&LocalInterruptDisabled> for SpinLockIrqBackend {
    type ActingBackend = SpinLockBackend;
}

although, I do wonder if this should actually be preferred vs just an inherent method on Lock<T, SpinLockIrqBackend>.

so are we just dropping globalguard support then?

GlobalLock is possible with something like

struct GlobalSpinLockIrqBackendAdapter<T>(T);

impl<B: GlobalLockBackend<Backend = SpinLockIrqBackend>> GlobalLockBackend for GlobalSpinLockIrqBackendAdapter<B> {
    const NAME: &'static  CStr = B::NAME;
    type Item = B::Item;
    type Backend = SpinLockBackend;

    fn get_lock_class() -> Pin<&'static LockClassKey> {
        B::get_lock_class()
    }
}

impl<B: GlobalLockBackend<Backend = SpinLockIrqBackend>> for GlobalLock<B>{
    pub fn lock_with(self: &'static Self) -> GlobalGuard<GlobalSpinLockIrqBackendAdapter<B>> { ... }
}

Although I might avoid adding this if there's no need yet.

BTW, global lock is also something that I am trying to get rid of -- it requires unsafe to initialize and does not drop the content on module unload. However I am still experimenting the exact replacement.
